channel.title: Wasil Zafar — Philosophy
channel.description: Latest philosophy articles from Wasil Zafar

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 9: Advanced Concepts
item.description: Advanced Eastern philosophy — sunyata (emptiness), pratityasamutpada (dependent origination), Madhyamaka two truths, non-dual awareness, Eastern virtue ethics, and phenomenological convergences.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 3: Buddhism
item.description: Buddhism — the life of Siddhartha Gautama, Four Noble Truths, Noble Eightfold Path, anatta (no-self), the major schools (Theravada, Mahayana, Vajrayana, Zen), and meditation.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 6: Comparative
item.description: Comparative Eastern philosophy — how Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism contrast on the self, society, suffering, and practice; and how Chinese culture integrated all three.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 5: Confucianism
item.description: Confucianism — Confucius, the Analects, the cardinal virtues (ren, li, xiao, yi, zhi), the project of self-cultivation, social harmony, and Mencius vs Xunzi on human nature.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 7: East-West Dialogue
item.description: East-West philosophical dialogue — substance vs process, autonomous self vs relational self, deductive logic vs paradox, theoretical knowledge vs transformative practice.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 2: Foundations
item.description: Foundations of Eastern philosophy — the unity of mind and world, impermanence, harmony, non-dual thinking, and the methodological style that distinguishes Asian traditions.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 10: Modern Applications
item.description: Modern applications of Eastern philosophy — clinical mindfulness and CBT, leadership, business strategy, the East Asian developmental model, and AI ethics in 2026.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 8: Practical Philosophy
item.description: Practical Eastern philosophy — daily meditation, ethical living, emotional mastery, and integrating Buddhist, Taoist, and Confucian practice into a contemporary life.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 4: Taoism
item.description: Taoism — Laozi and the Tao Te Ching, Zhuangzi
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 12: AI & Machines
item.description: AI and the philosophy of mind — Turing test, Searle
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 14: Applications
item.description: Applied philosophy of mind — neuroethics, criminal responsibility and the brain, mental health and psychiatric categories, brain-computer interfaces, and the moral status of AI.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 5: Consciousness
item.description: The Hard Problem of consciousness — qualia, philosophical zombies, the explanatory gap, Global Workspace Theory, and Integrated Information Theory.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 9: Emotions
item.description: Philosophy of emotions — James-Lange feeling theory, cognitive theories (Solomon, Nussbaum), basic emotions vs constructionism (Barrett), and emotions as moral knowledge.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 8: Free Will
item.description: Free will — hard determinism, libertarian free will, compatibilism (Hume, Frankfurt, Strawson), Frankfurt cases, Libet
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 4: Functionalism
item.description: Functionalism in philosophy of mind — mental states defined by their causal-functional roles. Multiple realizability, Putnam, Fodor, machine functionalism, and the Chinese Room critique.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 6: Intentionality
item.description: Intentionality — the
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 13: Modern Debates
item.description: Modern debates in philosophy of mind — embodied & extended cognition (4E), the panpsychism revival (Goff, Strawson), Integrated Information Theory, predictive processing, and enactivism.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 10: Perception
item.description: Philosophy of perception — direct vs indirect realism, the argument from illusion, sense-data theory, adverbialism, representationalism, disjunctivism, and predictive processing.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 7: Personal Identity
item.description: Personal identity over time — Locke
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 3: Physicalism
item.description: Physicalism in philosophy of mind — behaviorism, identity theory, eliminative materialism, and the famous arguments (Nagel
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 11: Self-Knowledge
item.description: Self-knowledge in philosophy of mind — privileged access, introspection, transparency, the constructive nature of self-reports, confabulation, and self-deception.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 10: Applied Political Philosophy
item.description: Applied political philosophy — policy design under value pluralism, criminal justice and punishment theory, economic regulation, public administration, and judging institutions by ideal vs comparative standards.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 6: Equality, Rights & Justice
item.description: Equality, rights, and justice — kinds of equality (formal, of opportunity, of outcome), distributive justice debates, the structure of rights, human rights theory, and historical injustice.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 7: Political Ideologies
item.description: The major political ideologies — classical and modern liberalism, conservatism, socialism, social democracy, libertarianism, anarchism, fascism — mapped against the building blocks of liberty, equality, and authority.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 2: Justice
item.description: Justice in political philosophy — Plato
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 3: Liberty & Freedom
item.description: Liberty in political philosophy — Isaiah Berlin
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 9: Freedom in Modern Systems
item.description: Freedom in modern systems — surveillance capitalism (Zuboff), platform power, algorithmic governance, AI ethics, the political economy of attention, and the structural threats to liberty in 2026.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 8: Modern Political Philosophy
item.description: Modern political philosophy — global justice, feminist political theory, postcolonial and decolonial thought, critical theory and the Frankfurt School, and contemporary democratic theory.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 4: Power, Authority & State
item.description: Power, authority, and the state — Weber on legitimacy, Hobbes on sovereignty, Marx on class power, Foucault on disciplinary and biopower, Arendt on political action.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 11: Research & Mastery
item.description: Research and mastery in political philosophy — methods (analytic, historical, critical, empirical), how to read primary texts, how to write argument, the indispensable canon, and capstone questions for self-test.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 5: Social Contract
item.description: The social contract tradition — Hobbes
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 1: Orientation
item.description: An accessible orientation to Eastern Philosophy. Discover what makes Eastern thought distinct, its three great traditions (Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism), and the questions that drive it.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism Part 11: Advanced Topics
item.description: Closing the existentialism series — absurdism vs nihilism, lived vs measured time, being-toward-death, narrative identity, and existentialism in the age of AI.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 4: Albert Camus — The Absurd & Revolt
item.description: Albert Camus on the Absurd, the Myth of Sisyphus, the three responses to absurdity, the absurd hero, and the strange affirmation that
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 5: Core Existential Themes
item.description: The recurring themes of existentialism across thinkers: freedom and responsibility, anxiety and dread, finitude and death, meaning-making, alienation, and authenticity vs inauthenticity.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 7: Existential Ethics
item.description: Existential ethics: morality without God, created values, radical responsibility, the difference between existential and neurotic guilt, Beauvoir
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 1: Foundations
item.description: An accessible introduction to Existentialism and the Absurd. Explore individual existence, freedom, meaning-making, and the philosophical movement that shaped 20th-century thought.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 6: Key Thinkers
item.description: The wider existential canon: Nietzsche on the death of God and the Übermensch, Heidegger on Dasein and Being-toward-death, Beauvoir
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 2: Søren Kierkegaard — The Birth of Existential Thought
item.description: Søren Kierkegaard, the lonely Danish thinker who fathered existentialism. Explore subjectivity, the three stages of existence, despair, the dizziness of freedom, and the leap of faith.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 9: Literature, Theater & Film
item.description: Existentialism and the Absurd in literature, theater, and film: Camus
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism Part 10: Modern Applications
item.description: Existentialism in the 21st century — career anxiety, social media identity, climate dread, AI existential risk, burnout as inauthenticity, and authentic leadership.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 8: Existential Psychology
item.description: Existential psychology: Yalom
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 3: Jean-Paul Sartre — Radical Freedom & Bad Faith
item.description: Jean-Paul Sartre on radical freedom, existence precedes essence, bad faith (mauvaise foi), the famous waiter, the look (le regard), and the demanding ideal of authenticity.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 2: Dualism
item.description: Substance dualism from Plato to Descartes — the cogito, the pineal gland, the interaction problem, property dualism, and Chalmers
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 1: Foundations
item.description: An introduction to Philosophy of Mind. Explore the core questions about consciousness, the mind-body problem, the self, and whether machines can think.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Political Philosophy Part 1: Foundations
item.description: An accessible introduction to Political Philosophy. Explore the central questions about justice, liberty, power, authority, and the legitimacy of the state.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 6: Applied Ethics & Case Studies
item.description: Apply ethical principles to real-world moral dilemmas. Explore bioethics, environmental ethics, business ethics, AI ethics, and work through contemporary case studies that challenge our moral reasoning. Applied ethics takes the normative theories we've studied and brings them to bear on specific
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 3: Deontology & Kant
item.description: Customize Settings Cookie Preferences Essential Cookies (Required) Necessary for the website to function properly. Analytics Cookies Help us understand how you interact with the website. Marketing Cookies Used to deliver relevant advertisements. Accept All Reject All Save Preferences Wasil Zafar
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 1: Foundations of Ethics
item.description: Customize Settings Cookie Preferences Essential Cookies (Required) Necessary for the website to function properly. Analytics Cookies Help us understand how you interact with the website. Marketing Cookies Used to deliver relevant advertisements. Accept All Reject All Save Preferences Wasil Zafar
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 5: Moral Relativism & Nihilism
item.description: Explore moral relativism, cultural relativism, and moral nihilism. Examine the profound challenges to objective morality and whether ethics can truly be universal or is inevitably culturally determined. Moral relativism is the view that moral judgments are not objectively true or false, but are
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)
